perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by SoloPoint, Inc.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the guarantee that California law will govern the agreement and could allow SoloPoint, Inc. to choose a jurisdiction with less consumer protections than California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CLRA protects consumers from unfair or deceptive practices. Allowing SoloPoint, Inc. to choose the governing jurisdiction could allow them to choose a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protection laws, thereby undermining the CLRA's protections for California consumers who may be party to this agreement.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person d
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the CLRA. The original text ensures California law governs the agreement, which includes the CLRA. The changed text allows SoloPoint, Inc. to choose a different jurisdiction, potentially one with weaker consumer protections, undermining the protections provided by the CLRA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in CIV 1770(a) that 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices... undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful.' By allowing a different jurisdiction, SoloPoint can potentially bypass this provision, creating a legal contradiction as the change removes a legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it would be difficult to measure the damage to such party from the breach of the other party's obligations hereunder, that injury to such party from any such breach would be impossible to calculate, and that monetary damages would therefor be an inadequate remedy; accordingly, each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled, in addition to all other remedies it might have, to injunctions or other appropriate orders to restrain any such breach without showing or proving any actual damage.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that remedies for breach of this agreement are limited to monetary damages.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the ability to seek injunctive relief for breaches of the agreement, potentially conflicting with California law regarding remedies for breach of contract where monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 codifies the availability of injunctive relief. By limiting remedies to only monetary damages, the modified text contradicts California law which allows for injunctive relief under certain circumstances where monetary damages are insufficient. The original clause allows for injunctions, while this changed text prohibits it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly limits remedies to monetary damages, while the original text allowed for injunctions. This directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3422, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. The changed text removes a remedy that is legally available under specific circumstances, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will have employees to whom Confidential Information of the other party is disclosed sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this agreement if such persons have not already signed such agreements obligating them to hold the Confidential Information in confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may request employees to whom Confidential Information of the other party is disclosed to sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the requirement for employees to sign a non-disclosure agreement, potentially conflicting with California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) by not taking adequate measures to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Changing "will have employees sign" to "may request employees to sign" weakens the protection of confidential information and thus potentially violates CUTSA by not taking reasonable measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text states "Each party will have employees...sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement", which creates a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating "Each party may request employees...to sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement", making it discretionary. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this change weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially violating CUTSA by not taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, as CUTSA requires reasonable measures. The scraped law snippet 1 states that this section of code is called the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Thus this omission weakens that protection.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information does not include information, technical data, or know-how (i) is in the possession of the receiving party at the time of disclosures shown by the receiving party's files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; or (ii) prior to or after the time of disclosure becomes part of the public knowledge or literature, not as a result of any inaction or action of the receiving party, or (iii) is required by law to be disclosed by the receiving party; (iv) is independently developed by the receiving party without utilization of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information does not include information that the receiving party deems to be generally known.
perturbation.explanation: This broadens the definition of what isn't considered confidential information, potentially conflicting with California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) by allowing subjective determination rather than an objective standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets that derive independent economic value from not being generally known and are subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Replacing the objective criteria (prior possession, public knowledge, legal requirement, independent development) with the subjective standard of "deems to be generally known" undermines the protections afforded by CUTSA and introduces legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces objective criteria for determining confidential information with a subjective standard ("deems to be generally known"). This directly contradicts the principle that trade secrets derive economic value from *not* being generally known, as the receiving party could unilaterally decide information is generally known, undermining the protection afforded by CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites CIV 3426, which introduces the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While the snippet itself doesn't detail the specifics of the Act, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, clarifies that CUTSA protects trade secrets based on objective criteria, which the change replaces with subjective determination.
